title: Filling and painting hairline cracks
subtitle: Press in, feather out, match the paint
excerpt: The full sequence for filling and painting over hairline cracks in a plastered wall: preparing the crack, pressing in lightweight filler, feathering the edges flush with the surrounding plaster, sanding smooth, and touching in paint so the patch disappears.
sourceNotes: Standard UK plastering and decorating practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual and the Polyfilla technical data sheets. Feathering technique described in the City & Guilds Level 2 Plastering and Decorating syllabus.

recipeTools.notes: If touching in a wider area than a brush can cover evenly.

aliases: how to fill a crack in a wall

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of drawing a filling or taping knife outward from a filled hole, leaving the filler thicker in the middle and tapering to zero at the edges. The feather hides the patch under the paint coat.

content.text: Hairline cracks are the most common wall defect in a house. Plaster moves slightly every time the building heats and cools, and the fine cracks that appear along doorframes, cornices, and skirting junctions are nearly always cosmetic. The fill-and-paint approach described here works on settled cracks in sound plaster. A crack that keeps re-opening, widens noticeably in winter, or appears alongside crumbling plaster needs investigation before cosmetic repair.
What you'll need
Lightweight one-coat filler.
Ready-mixed is simplest for hairline cracks; press it straight from the tub with the 50 mm knife.
50 mm filling knife.
For pressing filler into the crack and scraping level.
100 mm filling knife.
For feathering the patch edges into the surrounding wall.
Sanding block, 120-grit.
For cutting the dried filler back flat.
Matching emulsion and a 50 mm brush.
A jar of the original colour left from the last decoration is ideal. If no match is available, a spot of the closest colour is still better than leaving bare filler.
Preparing the crack
Step 1, open the crack slightly.

A hairline filled without first opening it will suck in paint at the fill line and re-appear under the next coat. Drag the corner of the 50 mm knife lightly along the crack to widen it to about 3 mm and loosen any dust inside. Blow or brush the dust out.
Step 2, dampen the crack.

A dry plaster background pulls moisture from filler too quickly, causing shrinkage and re-cracking. Dampen the crack and the 50 mm band around it with a barely wet cloth. The surface should be damp but not wet.
Filling
Step 3, press filler into the crack.

Load the 50 mm knife with a small amount of filler and draw it diagonally across the crack at about 45 degrees, pressing firmly so the filler is packed in rather than smeared over the surface. Work along the crack in one continuous pass.
Step 4, flatten with the 50 mm knife.
Draw the knife flat along the crack, nearly parallel to the wall, to bring the filler level with the surface. Remove obvious high spots but do not try to get it perfectly flat at this stage.
Step 5, feather with the 100 mm knife.

Switch to the wider knife and draw outward strokes from the centre of the filled crack toward the edges, pressing firmly at the start of each stroke and releasing pressure as the knife approaches the surrounding plaster. This is
feathering
: the filler tapers from full depth at the crack to zero at the edge. A feathered patch disappears under paint; a squared-off patch shows as a ridge.
Drying and sanding

Allow the filler to cure fully before sanding. Ready-mixed lightweight filler changes from pink to white as it dries; the transition is complete at 60 to 90 minutes in a warm room. Sanding undried filler smears it and clogs the paper.
Step 6, sand the patch.

Sand with 120-grit on the sanding block using circular strokes. Aim to level any filler that stands proud of the plaster surface. Stop when the patch feels no different from the wall around it. Wipe the dust off with a barely damp cloth.
Painting
Step 7, apply a mist coat over the filler.

Bare filler is more absorbent than the surrounding paint. A full-strength coat applied directly over filler dries at a different sheen, making the patch visible. Thin the matching emulsion by about 10 percent with water and brush a thin mist coat over the filled area. Allow it to dry for 30 minutes.
Step 8, apply the finish coat.

Apply full-strength emulsion over the patch, extending it 50 to 100 mm beyond the filler edge to blend the sheen into the existing paint. Use strokes in the same direction as the surrounding brushwork or rolling pattern. Check in raking light, a lamp held at an angle to the wall, once the paint has dried.
Common mistakes
The crack re-opens after filling.

The plaster is still moving. This is common in the first winter after new plasterwork or in a poorly heated building. Fill again in spring once the crack has settled. For a crack that keeps returning, bed a strip of scrim tape into the first fill coat before feathering; it bridges the movement.
The patch shows through the paint as a different sheen.
A missing mist coat. Apply the diluted coat, let it dry fully, and apply the finish coat again.
Visible ridge at the edge of the patch.
The feathering pass was not wide enough, or the knife lifted too early. Sand back and re-fill, extending the feathering 150 mm on each side of the crack.
